behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: Manifest validation is a pure function that checks data structure against a schema. No plugin code execution is required—the loader must validate and reject the manifest before attempting to load the plugin. Mocking can fully verify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call the manifest validator with a manifest missing a required field (e.g., no 'name' field). Verify it throws a validation error with a clear message. Confirm the error is raised synchronously before any loader attempt to require() the plugin module.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: Testing that B's onLoad actually runs before A's requires loading multiple real plugin modules and observing actual hook invocation. A unit test of the dependency-resolution algorithm alone cannot catch bugs where the resolver is correct but hooks execute in the wrong order, or where async handling breaks the ordering.
behaviors.test_description: Create two test plugin modules with manifests where A depends on B. Use a hook tracker (e.g., a global array or spy) to record when each onLoad is invoked. Load all plugins via the loader. Assert that B's onLoad completes (or at least begins) before A's onLoad begins.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Must verify both that the cycle is detected and reported, and that neither plugin's onLoad hook is actually invoked. A unit test of cycle-detection algorithm would only verify the detection logic, not the actual behavior of the loader when encountering a cycle. Integration testing with real plugin loading is needed to confirm neither plugin executes.
behaviors.test_description: Create two plugins with mutual dependencies (A depends on B, B depends on A). Invoke the loader, capture its error/log output. Verify a cycle error is reported. Track onLoad hook invocations for both plugins and confirm neither hook was called.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: Requires real Node.js module loading via require(). Mocking require() could hide failures where the real module-level code is not executing (e.g., a module has syntax errors or side effects that never run). Must use actual .js files and real import to verify module execution happens before onLoad invocation.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test plugin whose module-level code sets a flag in a global object (e.g., `global.pluginModuleLoaded = true`). In the plugin's onLoad hook, assert that this flag is already true. Load the plugin via the loader and verify the assertion passes, proving module-level code ran first.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: Must test three things simultaneously: (1) error handling doesn't crash the loader, (2) plugins with dependencies on the failed plugin don't load, (3) unrelated plugins continue to load. This requires the full plugin dependency graph and actual loading of multiple plugins. Unit-level error handling tests would miss the dependency-aware partial loading.
behaviors.test_description: Create three plugins: B throws in onLoad, A depends on B, C has no dependencies. Load all plugins. Verify: B's error is caught and logged, A's onLoad is never called (respects the failed dependency), C's onLoad completes successfully. Confirm the loader returns partial-success state rather than crashing.
